coredata.dc:title: Bio-inspired shape-memory structural color hydrogel film
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Science China Press. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science China Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science China Press. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science China Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yu
Zhang, Zhuohao
Chen, Hanxu
Zhang, Han
Zhang, Hui
Zhao, Yuanjin
coredata.dc:description: Structural colors, derived from existing natural creatures, have aroused widespread attention in the materials regulation for different applications. Here, inspired by the color adjusting mechanism of hummingbird, we present a novel shape-memory structural color hydrogel film by introducing shape memory polymers (SMPs) into synthetic inverse opal scaffold structure. The excellent flexibility as well as the inverse opal structure of the hydrogel films imparts them with stable stretchability and brilliant structural colors. Benefiting from the transient structural anisotropy of copolymers, the hybrid films are possessed with shape-morphing behaviors capability. Based on the shape transformations and color responsiveness performance, we have demonstrated diverse structural color actuators with complex shapes for different tasks. Notably, as the photothermal responsive graphene quantum dots were integrated into the hydrogel, the hybrid films could also be endowed with the feature of light-controlled reversible deformation with synchronous structural color variation. These features demonstrate that the presented shape-memory structural color hydrogel film is valuable for soft robotics with multi-functions of sensing, communication and disguise.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-inspired
Structural color
Shape-memory
Hydrogel
Responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The construction and programmable process of patterned SMSC hydrogel films

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Science China Press. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science China Press. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Color online) Scheme of bio-inspired SMSC hydrogels films. Hummingbird changes its feathers’ structural color by frequently turning their heads. The structural color variation of SMSC hydrogels films was induced by the shape transformation between heating/NIR and cooling process.

(Color online) Scheme and characterization of SMSC hydrogel film. (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation process of SMSC hydrogel film. SEM images of (b) colloidal crystal template, (c) the copolymers hydrogel-infiltrated colloidal crystal, and (d) SMSC hydrogel film with an inverse opal structure, respectively. (e) Mechanical properties of the SMSC hydrogel films with various concentrations of SA. (f) The reflection peak variations during the preparation process of SMSC hydrogel films. Scale bar is 500 nm.

(Color online) Shape transformation and color sensing capability of a snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film. (a) Schematics for the programmable process of the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film. (b) Optical images of the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film in 45 ℃ water. The bending angles in (b): (i) 0°, (ii) 30°, (iii) 50°, (iv) 80°, (v) 90°. (c) Bending-unbending cycles of the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film in different temperature water (45 and 15 ℃). (d) Relationship between the reflection wavelength shift value and the bending angle. Scale bar is 0.5 cm.

(Color online) The SMSC hydrogel films with more complex shapes. Schematic diagram and optical images of the various programmable SMSC hydrogel films with (a) cube box, (b) octopus, and (c) lotus. Scale bar is 1 cm.

(Color online) Photocontrollable performance of a butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel film. (a) Optical images of butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel films surface during NIR irradiation. (b) Optical images of butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel films under NIR irradiation. (c) The relationship between the butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel films bending angle and NIR irradiation time under different NIR power. (d) Bending angle changes during repeated cycles when the butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel films were switched under NIR light irradiation. Irradiation time in (a) and (b): t1, 0 s; t2, 10 s; t3, 15 s; t4, 20 s. Scale bar is 1 cm.

(Color online) The applications of the paw-shaped SMSC hydrogel film. Schematic (a) and optical images (b) of the paw-shaped SMSC hydrogel film applied as a soft actuator to grab a glass ball in 45 ℃ hot water (t1: 10 s; t2: 60 s; t3: 90 s; t4: 120 s). Schematic (c) and optical images (d) of the SMSC hydrogel film applied as intelligent dynamic anti-counterfeiting labels for products (i: 0 s; ii: 10 s; iii: 15 s; iv: 20 s). Scale bar is 1 cm.

head.ce:title: Bio-inspired shape-memory structural color hydrogel film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Institute of Translational Medicine, The Affiliated Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing 210008, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Institute of Translational Medicine, The Affiliated Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing 210008, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Biomedicine Innovation Center, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Biomedicine Innovation Center, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel shape-memory structural color hydrogel film is presented by introducing shape memory polymers (SMPs) into synthetic inverse opal scaffold structure. The film not only exhibits the shape transformations and color responsiveness performance, but also possesses the feature of light-controlled reversible deformation with synchronous structural color variation, which has highly potential values in the field of soft robotics.

Structural colors, derived from existing natural creatures, have aroused widespread attention in the materials regulation for different applications. Here, inspired by the color adjusting mechanism of hummingbird, we present a novel shape-memory structural color hydrogel film by introducing shape memory polymers (SMPs) into synthetic inverse opal scaffold structure. The excellent flexibility as well as the inverse opal structure of the hydrogel films imparts them with stable stretchability and brilliant structural colors. Benefiting from the transient structural anisotropy of copolymers, the hybrid films are possessed with shape-morphing behaviors capability. Based on the shape transformations and color responsiveness performance, we have demonstrated diverse structural color actuators with complex shapes for different tasks. Notably, as the photothermal responsive graphene quantum dots were integrated into the hydrogel, the hybrid films could also be endowed with the feature of light-controlled reversible deformation with synchronous structural color variation. These features demonstrate that the presented shape-memory structural color hydrogel film is valuable for soft robotics with multi-functions of sensing, communication and disguise.

ce:section.ce:para: Structural colors, derived from the interaction between the periodic nanostructures and the specific wavelengths of light, widely exist in nature [1–4]. Due to their dazzling colors, scientists have been attracted by these natural examples and devoted themselves to develop various bio-inspired structural color materials [5–8]. Notably, based on the abilities of modulating electromagnetic waves and controlling the propagations of photons by using the energy in photonic band gaps (PBG), such bio-inspired structural color materials have been investigated and applied in optical materials, anticounterfeiting labels, and wearable devices [9–15]. Although many achievements have been made in modulating the structural color based on adjusting the lattice parametersincluding mechanically, thermally, electrically or magnetically stimuli, it is still challenging to simulate the spontaneous regulation of structural color behaviors of natural examples [16–19]. In addition, mimicking the angle regulation of natural creatures provides another effective way for structural color variation behavior, but it is rarely reported. Therefore, the development of a novel bioinspired structural color material that has a spontaneous angle regulation capability will be valuable in broadening next-generation smart photonic devices.

In this paper, inspired by the color adjusting mechanism of hummingbird, we propose a novel shape-memory structural color (SMSC) hydrogel film with the desired features, as schemed in Fig. 1 . Hummingbird changes its feathers’ structural color with the angle of the light, which is attributed to a combined scattering, refraction and interference of the incident light falling on the unique micro/nanostructures on the feather surface [20–23]. This structural color adjusting mechanism is interesting and meaningful, while there are few researches on its bionic investigations. In contrast, shape memory polymers (SMPs) could respond to external stimuli, manifested as the variations of shape, stiffness, size, and structure [24–28]. These features enable them show great potential in diverse applications, such as soft actuators, biomedical devices, and flexible electronics [29–32]. Although the traditional shape-memory actuators conduct well in shape deformation or movement, most of them have difficulty in acquiring more complex functions such as real-time monitoring of deformation behaviors, which might represent an obstacle to their application [33,34]. Thus, it might have excellent performances of both sensitive color and mechanical responses by imparting the structural colors into the SMPs, while only few examples of structural color actuators could achieve these features simultaneously.

Herein, we fabricated the desired shape-memory structural color film by introducing SMPs of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) and stearyl acrylate (SA) copolymers into synthetic inverse opal scaffold structures for template replicating. Based on the remarkable flexibility and the existing of inverse opal structure, the resultant film exhibited good mechanical strength and brilliant structural color. Meanwhile, the resultant film presented angle-dependence structural color performance due to the effect of Bragg diffraction. In addition, owing to the transient structural anisotropy of copolymers, the film was endowed with shape-morphing behaviors capability. Taking the advantages of shape transformations and color responsiveness performance, diverse structural color actuators with complex shapes, such as cube box, octopus and lotus could be designed, achieving the dynamic modulation of structural color. Attractively, by incorporating photothermal responsive graphene quantum dots (QDs), the materials could be imparted with light-controlled reversible deformation with synchronous structural color variation. These distinctive performance of the shape-memory structural color hydrogel films indicated their practically valuable in sensing, communication, and disguise of soft robotics.

SiO2 nanoparticles with sizes of 250, 270 and 290 nm were fabricated by ourselves. NIPAM, SA, 2,2′-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (BIS) were bought from Sigma Aldrich. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), hydrofluoric acid (HF), 4 Å molecular sieves were acquired from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. 4 Å molecular sieves were used to remove water in DMSO before use. Graphene QDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 10% was acquired from Nanjing XF NANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. Deionized water (18 MΩ cm) was obtained by a Milli-Q Plus 185 water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA).

The SMSC hydrogel films with inverse opal scaffold structure were acquired from a sacrificial template method. The colloidal crystal template was acquired from self-assembling silica nanoparticles, which originated from a 4 wt% silica nanoparticles ethanol solution by vertical deposition on the glass slides. The obtained colloidal crystal templates were calcined in a muffle furnace (600 ℃) for 4 h, strengthening their mechanical property. Afterwards, a pregel solution was prepared, consisting of NIPAM, 0.06% molar ratio of SA, 2.5% molar ratio of N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) and DMSO (4 mL). The total mass of NIPAM and SA was set as 1 g. After the addition of initiator AIBN (0.01 g), the pregel solution was filled into the colloidal crystal templates. By placing a pair of 0.5 mm silicone spacers on both ends of the silica colloidal crystal templates, the SMSC hydrogel films of fixed thickness could be achieved. The pregel solution infiltrated colloidal crystal template was kept in an oven at 75 °C for 8 h to induce radical copolymerization. After polymerization, the SMSC hydrogel films were acquired by putting the cured pregel solution infiltrated colloidal crystal template into HF to remove the templates. In the process of shape deformation, the bending angles of the films at a given time were taken and further calculated by digital photos.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction and programmable process of patterned SMSC hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: By using embossing molds, different patterned SMSC hydrogel films were fabricated, including snowflake, cube box, octopus, lotus, and paw. For snowflake, cube box, and paw-shaped SMSC hydrogel films, they were fixed temporary shapes consisting of wrapping, folding and shrinking in 45 ℃ water for 2 min. After cooling in 15 ℃ water for 2 h, they were recovered to their original shapes. For octopus-shaped SMSC hydrogel film, the paw part of the octopus was immersed in 45 ℃ water for 2 min to program a deformable shape, while the rest part of octopus remained unchanged. Next, the recovery of the whole octopus was realized by touching with 15 ℃ water for 2 h. For lotus-shaped SMSC hydrogel film, the bottom, middle, and upper parts of the lotus were fixed at different angles by molds in 45 ℃ water for 2 min. Afterwards, the whole lotus was placed in 15 ℃ water for 2 h to cool to the original state.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Ultra Plus, Zeiss) was adopted to characterize the microstructures of the SMSC hydrogel films. The mechanical strengths of the materials were detected by Single Column Motor Table (500, HSV). The reflection spectra of materials were examined through an optical microscope (Olympus, BX51), combined with a fiber-optic spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USB2000-FLG). The photographs of the samples were acquired by a digital camera (Canon 5D Mark II). The near-infrared irradiation (NIR, 808 nm, Xi Long Tech Co., Ltd.) was used to study the photo-controllable performance of the materials.

In a typical experiment, the SMSC hydrogel films were prepared by the template sacrifice method for the construction of the inverse opal-structured hydrogel film. First, the colloidal crystal templates were fabricated by self-assembling silica crystal particles on the planer glass surface through the gradual evaporation of the solvent. Driven by capillary force, a well-ordered close packing template with a hexagonal organization feature appeared (Fig. 2 ai, b). Normally, the mechanical strength of the acquired templates was improved by treating them at 600 °C. Then, a pregel solution of copolymers consisting of NIPAM, SA, and MBA was produced and filled into the templates. Taking the advantage of capillary action, voids between the silica nanoparticles could be fully immersed with the solution. After polymerizing under 80 ℃ for 8 h, a solidified composited hydrogel with the colloidal crystal embedded was formed, as demonstrated in Fig. 2aii, c. Finally, a free-standing SMSC hydrogel film with the periodic and ordered porous structures was acquired after removing silica templates (Fig. 2aiii, d).

The resultant SMSC hydrogel films were imparted with outstanding flexibility, which was investigated through the characterization of mechanical properties, as shown in Fig. 2e. It was found that with the increase of the SA concentrations, the mechanical strength of the SMSC hydrogel films was enhanced, which may be attributed to hydrophobic interactions leading to the denser physical crosslinking. Compared with the original shape-memory hydrogels, the inverse opal structure of the SMSC hydrogel films had a slight impact on the mechanical strength (Fig. S1 online). In addition, resulting from the periodic arrangement of internal microstructures, SMSC hydrogel films showed unique optical performance, that is photonic band gap (PBG). As incident light is shining the material, the presence of the microstructure will affect the transmission of the light so that only light with a specific frequency could be reflected. Thus, the SMSC hydrogel films could present vivid structural color. And the color of the shape-memory film was not sensitive to humidity change. The PBG position (λ) of light with a specific frequency could be determined by the Bragg’s equation [35]:
(1)
m λ = 1.633
d
111
n
a v e r a g e
2
- s i
n 2
θ
1 / 2
,
where m refers to the order of diffraction, d 111 is the interplanar distance of the (111) diffracting planes, n average means the average refractive index of SMSC hydrogel films, and θ is the incident angle falling on the nanostructures. The reflection peaks of the SMSC hydrogel films could be detected (Fig. 2f). It could be seen that as the SMSC hydrogel films obtained, the reflection peak was red-shifted, which could be attributed to the increase of refractive index. Notably, it could be indicated from the equation that when the ingredient of materials keeps invariant, n average is constant. Therefore, λ is dependent on d 111 and θ, which can be regulated by the size of colloidal crystal nanoparticles and the incident angles. Thus, with the size of colloidal crystal nanoparticles increasing, λ was increased. Besides, as the incident angle increased at the direction parallel to the long axis of the scale, λ was decreased, showing a blue-shifted trend.

Because of the melting transition of stearyl segments and the thermal responsiveness of the poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) chains which bring about the volume phase transition (VPT), the SMSC hydrogel film was endowed with shape morphing behaviors. In the system, both the cooling-induced shape fixation and heating-induced recovery were based on the melting transition stearyl segments. Meanwhile, PNIPAM displayed an opposite shape-memory behavior, in which its VPT was caused by its lower critical solution transition (LCST). By adjusting LCST, heating-induced shape fixing and cooling-induced recovery could be realized. Combining two polymers could achieve a synergistic interaction with two transitions to control the shape transition behavior. And the presence of hydrophobic stearyl segments exceeded VPT temperature and promoted the transformations of PNIPAM chains from a coil state to globular state, resulting from the hydrophobic interactions. Due to the important role of SA in the shape transformation performance, the SMSC hydrogel films with various SA concentrations were prepared and their shape-changing capability was studied. In detail, the SMSC hydrogel films with various SA concentrations were folded and immersed in 45 ℃ water to fix a temporary structure, followed by cooling in 15 ℃ water to recover to their original shapes for 2 h. Subsequently, the programmed SMSC hydrogels with various SA concentrations were touched with 45 ℃ water and their shape-memory responsiveness was explored. It could be found that all the SMSC hydrogels with various SA concentrations exhibited good shape-memory property, in which higher SA concentrations showed faster shape-memory responsiveness (Fig. S2a online). And when the SA concentration of the SMSC hydrogel film was set as 0.10, its shape-memory process could be accomplished in 1 min. In addition, the actuation angles of the SMSC hydrogel films with various SA concentrations in 2 min were recorded (Fig. S2b online). It was shown that the programmed SMSC hydrogel film (SA-0.08) displayed a reversible angle variation of ≈160°, which was significantly better than other concentrations of SMSC hydrogel films. Notably, whether it is the SMSC hydrogel films with fewer stearyl groups (SA-0.2) or with too many stearyl groups (e.g., SA-0.10), no good angle actuation was observed. However, because of the presence of SA affecting the transparency, the SMSC hydrogel film with SA-0.06 was applied for the subsequent experiment.

Additionally, to further explore the shape-changing capability and structural color responsive performance, a snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film was prepared. The snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film was programmed via folding it in 45 ℃ water for 2 min to fix a temporary wrapping shape (Fig. 3 a). Next, the film was placed in water at 15 ℃ to restore to its original shape. The programmable snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film could achieve a reversible bending-unbending variation according to the temperature changes. It could be found in Fig. 3b that in 45 ℃ water, the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film showed a bending process and the bending angle variations were recorded. Meanwhile, the reversible bending-unbending variations of the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel films during five cycles were investigated in the condition of the temperature changes from 45 to 15 ℃ (Fig. 3c). It could be demonstrated that when immersed in 45 ℃ water for about 2 min, the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film bent from 0° to nearly 135°. While immersed in 15 ℃ water for 2 h, it could gradually recover to 0°. This phenomenon was anticipated that the actuation was reversible. Furthermore, the multi-cycling bending-unbending test indicated that the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel films possessed good reversible deformability, which was illustrated by the statistics of the angle changes during five bending-unbending cycles (Fig. S3 online). Such reversible deformation change in response to temperature was caused by the transition of a random coiled conformation of the PNIPAM chain at low temperature to globule conformation at high temperature. Besides, it should be mentioned that from a fixed vertical viewing position, the structural color of the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film was varied from red to purple as the snowflake wrapped inward (Fig. 3b). This could be ascribed to the increase of the angle between the observation position and normal line of the snowflake-shaped SMSC hydrogel film during the bending process. According to Eq. (1), as the incident angle θ increased, λ was decreased correspondingly, showing a blue shift. Meanwhile, there were various colors in different parts of the same snowflake film, which was ascribed to different bending angles of different positions from the same snowflake film. In addition, the reflection peak displacement values induced by the dynamic bending angle were recorded (Fig. 3d). It could be found that with the increase of the bending angle, the reflection peak displacement values also gradually increased, which was consistent with the structural color changes. These results demonstrated that SMSC hydrogel film possessed good shape-memory capability and self-reporting performance.

Based on shape-memory capability and color-changing property responding to bending angle variations, shape-memory SMSC hydrogel films with more complex shapes could be designed, which was expected to broaden their practical values (Fig. 4 ). It could be found from Fig. 4a that the resulting cube box was made of programming the irregular strips of SMSC hydrogel film. When immersed in 45 ℃ water, the irregular strips of SMSC hydrogel film were bending up to the programmed position to form a preset graphic. It was worth mentioning that the structural color was transformed from red to blue during the process, showing excellent color-sensing ability. Interestingly, taking advantage of these features, by programming part of the object, it was possible to endow part of the object with the deformation capability. As expected, the paw area of the octopus-shaped SMSC hydrogel film was programmed and further bent (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, it showed color-changing upon the bending area, while the other regions remained constant. Besides, more complex objects such as lotus-shaped SMSC hydrogel films, whose petals possessed different bending angles and different structural colors, could also be achieved (Fig. 4c), which indicated that the SMSC hydrogel films could serve as a powerful platform for developing hydrogel-based intelligent soft devices.

To impart the SMSC hydrogel films with photo-controllable capability, graphene QDs, which exhibited excellent light-to-heat transformation efficiency, were introduced to the hydrogel films. To explore the remote controllability as well as the photothermal response performance, we created a butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel film. In detail, QDs ethanol solution would be scratched onto the SiO2 template twice. Followed by polymerized with shape-memory copolymers solution and etching, a SMSC hydrogel film with the photothermal capability was obtained. Next, a butterfly-shaped imprint was used to cut SMSC hydrogel film. Finally, the butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel film with remote controllability and photothermal responsive performance was acquired (Fig. 5 a). As the butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel film was continuously illuminated under near-infrared (NIR) light, a noticeable transformation has taken place that the butterfly began to bend, and the structural color showed a corresponding variation from red to purple. The corresponding reflection peaks were detected as well, displaying a blue shift with the increase of bending angle (Fig. S4a online). The bending behavior was presumably due to the temperature increase under NIR irradiation, which was illustrated by infrared imaging that the temperature was elevated from 22.6 to 49.4 ℃ (Fig. S5a–d online). Besides, during the process, the NIR response speed of the butterfly-shaped SMSC hydrogel film increased with the increase of irradiation power (Fig. 5c). Thus, the irradiation power of 5 W cm−2 was selected to apply for the SMSC hydrogel film for the subsequent experiments. In order to observe the phenomenon clearly, the snowflake-shaped SMSC film was irradiated by NIR light, showing good responsiveness (Fig. S6 online). And the relationship of the temperature and bending angle was evaluated that with the increase of the temperature under irradiation for 20 s, the bending angle was elevated gradually (Fig. S5e online). Furthermore, the stability and repeatability were investigated (Fig. 5d and Fig. S4b online). It could be inferred that SMSC hydrogel film showed good stability and repeatability of the photo-induced deformability and color variation even after five cycles. These results illustrated that the SMSC hydrogel film displayed brilliant remote controllability and excellent photothermal responsive performance.

Given the above-described remarkable features including shape-memory capability and color-sensing characteristic induced by angle changes, a paw-shaped SMSC hydrogel film was designed and then utilized as a soft actuator to grab glass balls in hot water (Fig. 6 a). When immersing in a 45 ℃ hot water to grab the glass balls (≈0.5 g), the paw-shaped SMSC hydrogel film would be deformed caused by temperature and display an evident structural color variation (Fig. 6b). In detail, as the paw-shaped SMSC hydrogel film gradually shrank and wrapped the glass ball from a flat shape, its structural color varied from red to blue-green. After about 2 min, the glass ball was completely covered by the paw and could be lifted up as well as transferred to other places. In addition, benefiting from the remote controllability and photothermal responsive properties, the SMSC hydrogel film could be applied for manipulating the surface morphology, as intelligent dynamic anti-counterfeiting labels (Fig. 6c). A pattern of “OK” was embossed into the structural color hydrogel film to fix a temporary shape at 45 ℃. After recovery for 2 h in 15 ℃ water, the pattern became invisible. When irradiated by NIR light, the “OK” pattern appeared again without external force (Fig. 6d). This could be ascribed to the heating of QDs triggered by NIR light, further causing the deformable responsiveness of the SMSC hydrogel film. At the same time, the structural color hydrogel film showed a reversible actuation performance. By relaxing in cold water for a period of time, the pattern on the surface of SMSC hydrogel film could be fully eliminated (Fig. S7 online). These results indicated that the SMSC hydrogel films and their use as the soft actuator and intelligent dynamic anti-counterfeiting labels would be of great beneficial in the field of smart hydrogel-based devices.

In summary, we have designed a bio-inspired shape-memory structural color film by introducing SMPs of NIPAM and SA into synthetic inverse opal scaffold structure. It was demonstrated that the resultant film was imparted with stable stretchability and brilliant structural color due to the excellent flexibility and inverse opal structure of the hydrogel film. Besides, based on the transient structural anisotropy of copolymers, the film was endowed with shape-morphing behaviors capability. Attractively, by incorporating photothermal responsive graphene QDs, the materials could be imparted with photocontrolled reversible deformation and structural color-changing behavior. Although the NIR laser triggering the shape and color change of photonic crystal polymers have been reported, SMSC hydrogel films possessed unique merits in shape transformations and color responsiveness performance, which could be designed into diverse complex objects, such as cube box, octopus, and lotus, achieving the dynamic modulation of structural color. These characteristics of the shape-memory structural color hydrogel films made them promising candidates for sensing, communication, and disguise of soft robotics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0908200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52073060 and 61927805), the Shenzhen Fundamental Research Program (JCYJ20190813152616459), and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation.

Yuanjin Zhao conceived the idea and designed the experiment. Yu Wang carried out the experiments. Yu Wang analyzed data and wrote the paper. Zhuohao Zhang, Hanxu Chen, Han Zhang, and Hui Zhang contributed to scientific discussion of the article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary materials to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scib.2021.10.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All-dielectric metasurface for high-performance structural color

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang W, Xiao S, Song Q, et al. All-dielectric metasurface for high-performance structural color. Nat Commun 2020; 11: 1864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution of ingle gyroid photonic crystals in bird feathers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Saranathan V, Narayanan S, Sandy A, et al. Evolution of ingle gyroid photonic crystals in bird feathers. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA. 2021; 118: e2101357118.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable structural color patterns based on the visible-light-responsive dynamic diselenide metathesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu C, Fan Z, Tan Y, et al. Tunable structural color patterns based on the visible-light-responsive dynamic diselenide metathesis. Adv Mater 2020; 32: 1907569.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright high-colour-purity deep-blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes via efficient edge amination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan F, Wang Y, Sharma G, et al. Bright high-colour-purity deep-blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes via efficient edge amination. Nat Photonics 2020; 14: 171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioinspired structural color patch with anisotropic surface adhesion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang Y, Shang L, Chen G, et al. Bioinspired structural color patch with anisotropic surface adhesion. Sci Adv 2020; 6: eaax8258.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioinspired bilayer structural color hydrogel actuator with multienvironment responsiveness and survivability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang Z, Chen Z, Wang Y, et al. Bioinspired bilayer structural color hydrogel actuator with multienvironment responsiveness and survivability. Small Methods 2019; 3: 1900519.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flower inspiration: broad-angle structural color through tunable hierarchical wrinkles in thin film multilayers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen C, Airoldi C A, Lugo C A, et al. Flower inspiration: broad-angle structural color through tunable hierarchical wrinkles in thin film multilayers. Adv Funct Mater 2020; 31: 2006256.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioinspired color switchable photonic crystal silicone elastomer kirigami

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lai X, Peng J, Cheng Q, et al. Bioinspired color switchable photonic crystal silicone elastomer kirigami. Angew Chem Int Ed 2021; 60: 14307-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bio-inspired stretchable, adhesive, and conductive structural color film for visually flexible electronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang Y, Yu Y, Guo J, et al. Bio-inspired stretchable, adhesive, and conductive structural color film for visually flexible electronics. Adv Funct Mater 2020; 31: 2000151.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomedical applications of gelatin methacryloyl hydrogels

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermo-adaptive block copolymer structural color electronics

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yu Wang received her doctor’s degree from School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University in 2021. Now, she worked as a postdoctoral fellow at the Affiliated Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School under the supervision of Prof. Yuanjin Zhao. Her current scientific interest is focused on functional materials and their biomedical applications.

Yuanjin Zhao is currently a full professor at the Medical School, Nanjing University. He received his Ph.D. degree in 2011 from the School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University. In 2009–2010, he worked as a research scholar in Prof. David A. Weitz’s group of Harvard University. His current scientific interest includes microfluidics, biomaterials, and organs-on-chips.
